narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 60% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your low ranking in Year 1 and Year 3 at school—both in the bottom decile compared to the typical student's middle-range performance—your notably low LSAT score of 23, which is well below the average of around 37, and your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, right around the dataset average. Your lower-middle family income bracket also factored in, as did your background as a Hispanic female planning full-time studies, though these are less common in the dataset where most students are white males from higher income groups attending full-time. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 school decile, which had the single biggest influence in pushing the prediction toward failure. Right behind that was your LSAT score, which significantly nudged the odds against passing. Your Year 1 decile came next in importance, reinforcing the concern about your early academic performance. Family income played a notable role too, followed by your undergraduate GPA, your racial background, gender, and full-time status, all of which contributed toward the failure prediction to lesser degrees, with none pulling strongly in the positive direction. Compared to typical students who pass—who often have much higher deciles, LSATs around 37, and stronger overall profiles—yours stood out in ways that raised red flags for bar success. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can realistically change, like retaking the LSAT to boost that critical score, as it's one of the top influencers and highly changeable with dedicated prep. Building a stronger academic record through additional coursework or certifications could help offset those low school deciles in future evaluations. Seeking scholarships or support programs tailored to lower-middle income or underrepresented students like yourself might also strengthen your application indirectly by showing commitment. With targeted effort on these areas, you could shift the model's view and your real-world bar outcomes toward success. Keep pushing forward—you have the potential to turn this around.
